summary: Merges a Filter relational operator into a underlying Calc operator by combining their RexPrograms, effectively absorbing the filter condition into the calculation logic to reduce operator depth and overhead.
ra_condition: π does not contain window functions (ω), filter p has no subqueries
correctness_conditions: The Calc operator must not contain windowed aggregates (containsOver() is false)
The Filter condition must not contain subqueries (checked via RexUtil.SubQueryFinder)
The merged RexProgram must preserve semantic equivalence of expressions and types
performance_impact: Generally beneficial by reducing operator count and iterator overhead; allows predicates to be evaluated within the calculation logic. Negligible negative impact unless the merged program becomes too complex for subsequent optimization passes.
edge_cases: Queries with window functions (OVER clause) in the Calc operator
Filters containing correlated subqueries or scalar subqueries
Scenarios where keeping the Filter separate might allow better predicate pushdown later
framework_notes: Belongs to VolcanoPlanner's logical optimization phase. It preserves the TraitSet of the Calc operator, ensuring physical properties like distribution and collation are maintained. It is a transformation rule that reduces the relational tree height.

summary: This rule derives IS NOT NULL filters from join conditions and pushes them down to the join's left and right inputs. For inner joins, if a column reference in the join condition must be non-null for the condition to evaluate to true, a filter is added to eliminate null values early in the execution plan.
ra_condition: Join is INNER JOIN, join condition q is not always true, and Strong.isNotTrue identifies columns that must be non-null
correctness_conditions: Join type must be INNER JOIN (outer joins preserve nulls and would change semantics)
Join condition must not be always true (no predicates to derive)
Columns identified as not-nullable must actually require non-null values for the condition to evaluate to true
The Strong predicate analysis must correctly identify which column nulls would make the condition not-true
performance_impact: Beneficial when null values exist in join columns, allowing earlier filtering and reducing join input size. May have minimal impact if columns are already constrained NOT NULL by schema, or if the cost of additional filter operators outweighs the benefit of reduced join size. Most effective when null rejection significantly reduces cardinality before expensive join operations.
edge_cases: Columns already defined as NOT NULL in schema (redundant filters add overhead)
Join conditions that don't require non-null values (e.g., IS NULL checks, COALESCE)
Very selective joins where filter overhead exceeds benefit
Outer joins (rule doesn't match these - would change semantics)
Join conditions with complex expressions where Strong analysis cannot determine null requirements
framework_notes: VolcanoPlanner rule using RelOptRuleCall pattern. Matches LogicalJoin with INNER type via Config.operand(). Uses RelBuilder to construct new filter operators on both inputs. Copies traitSet to maintain physical properties during transformation. Runs during logical optimization phase before physical planning. Uses RelMetadataQuery for predicate analysis.

summary: This rule pushes sort requirements down to the inputs of a Join operation while maintaining the original Sort operator on top. It decomposes the sort collation into left and right components, adding Sort operators to the Join inputs if they are not already sorted, facilitating potential Merge Join optimizations.
ra_condition: c⃗_L references columns from R, c⃗_R references columns from S, c⃗ is the concatenation of c⃗_L and c⃗_R
correctness_conditions: The top Sort operator preserves the global output order regardless of input sorting.
Field indices for the right input sort must be shifted by the row count of the left input.
Added Sort operators on inputs must not filter or modify data, only enforce physical ordering.
The Join type and condition must remain unchanged to preserve semantic equivalence.
performance_impact: Beneficial when it enables a Merge Join implementation which is more efficient than Hash Join for sorted data or memory-constrained environments. Potentially harmful if it introduces redundant sorting overhead without triggering a more efficient Join algorithm, though metadata checks minimize this risk.
edge_cases: Queries where one side of the join has no sort keys (empty collation list).
Inputs that are already sorted according to the required collation (rule detects and skips).
Outer joins where Merge Join semantics are more complex than Inner Joins.
Schemas with high cardinality where sorting cost outweighs Join benefits.
framework_notes: Belongs to VolcanoPlanner as a logical rule that interacts with the physical trait system (RelCollationTraitDef). It typically runs during the logical optimization phase to prepare the plan for physical rules like SortMergeJoinRule, relying on trait propagation to potentially remove the top Sort later.

summary: Pushes a Semi-Join operator down through a standard Join operator, applying the Semi-Join filter to one of the Join's inputs to reduce cardinality early, provided the join condition and type allow it.
ra_condition: Semi-Join condition references columns exclusively from X or Y; Join type allows pushing Semi-Join from above to the respective side
correctness_conditions: Semi-Join condition keys must originate from exactly one input of the lower Join (X or Y), not both
The lower Join type must support pushing predicates/Semi-Joins from above to the targeted input side (e.g., cannot push to right side of Left Outer Join)
The lower Join cannot itself be a Semi-Join
performance_impact: Generally beneficial by reducing cardinality of the lower Join's output before processing the other input; potentially negative if the Semi-Join execution cost outweighs the filtering benefit or if it prevents other optimization paths
edge_cases: Semi-Join condition references columns from both inputs of the lower Join
Lower Join is an Outer Join where pushing to the null-supplying side would change semantics
Lower Join is already a Semi-Join
framework_notes: VolcanoPlanner rule; preserves traits of the original Join via getTraitSet(); uses RexInputConverter to adjust field indices in the Semi-Join condition to match the new input ordering

summary: This rule inserts a Project operator between an Aggregate and its input (typically a TableScan) to prune unused columns, enabling further optimization like column pruning at the storage level.
ra_condition: R is not a Project, C is the set of columns referenced by grouping keys G and aggregate functions F
correctness_conditions: The mapping between original input field indices and projected field indices must be preserved correctly via the Mapping object
The set of projected columns C must strictly include all columns referenced by grouping keys and aggregate arguments
The input relation R must not already be a Project to avoid creating redundant projection chains
performance_impact: Highly beneficial when the input relation is wide (many columns) but the aggregate uses few, reducing memory bandwidth and CPU processing for unused columns. It enables storage-level column pruning if followed by a Project-Scan merge rule. Overhead of the extra Project node is negligible.
edge_cases: Queries where the Aggregate uses all input columns (Project becomes identity operation)
Inputs that do not support projection pushdown (e.g., certain custom RelNodes or complex subqueries)
Plans where a Project already exists below the Aggregate (prevented by rule predicate to avoid infinite firing)
framework_notes: Operates in the Logical plan phase of VolcanoPlanner. Designed to trigger subsequent rules like ProjectTableScanRule to merge the projection into the scan. Default configuration specifically targets Aggregate over LogicalTableScan to facilitate storage-level pruning.

summary: This rule optimizes a Sort operator by removing sort keys that are functionally determined by preceding keys in the sort list, reducing sorting overhead without changing the result order.
ra_condition: For every removed key kj, there exists a preceding kept key ki such that ki → kj (ki functionally determines kj)
correctness_conditions: RelMetadataQuery accurately identifies functional dependencies between columns
The functional dependency holds for all possible data values including NULLs as per SQL semantics
The reduced collation trait must satisfy any ordering requirements imposed by parent operators
The remaining sort keys preserve the necessary ordering for query semantics
performance_impact: Reduces CPU cost of sorting by decreasing the number of columns compared during sort operations. Minimal overhead for rule application.
edge_cases: Queries where functional dependencies are not captured in metadata
Sorts involving complex expressions rather than simple field references
Cases where downstream operators strictly require specific collation traits that might not recognize implicit ordering
Scenarios where sort stability is relied upon for non-determined keys
framework_notes: Usable by both HepPlanner and VolcanoPlanner as a RelOptRule. Typically applied during logical optimization phase. Interacts with trait system by simplifying RelCollation traits, relying on RelMetadataQuery for dependency analysis.

summary: Merges multiple branches of a distinct Set Operation (Union, Intersect, Minus) that scan the same underlying relation into a single scan with a combined filter condition, potentially eliminating the Set Operator entirely if all branches merge.
ra_condition: Union is distinct (not ALL), R is the same underlying relation, p and q are deterministic
correctness_conditions: Set operation must be distinct (all=false)
All merged inputs must reference the identical underlying relational node
Filter conditions must be deterministic (no random functions)
Filter conditions must not contain subqueries
Input schemas must be union-compatible
performance_impact: Beneficial when it reduces the number of table scans (e.g., scanning table T once instead of twice). May be detrimental if the combined predicate prevents efficient index usage compared to separate predicates.
edge_cases: UNION ALL operations are explicitly skipped
Inputs referencing different tables cannot be merged
Non-deterministic predicates (e.g., RAND()) prevent merging
Predicates containing subqueries prevent merging
framework_notes: Applies during logical optimization phase in VolcanoPlanner. Operates on logical RelNodes without physical trait requirements. Uses RelBuilder to reconstruct the plan.

summary: This rule transforms aggregate functions containing FILTER clauses into equivalent aggregate functions using CASE expressions within a preceding Project operator, effectively removing the FILTER syntax from the Aggregate call.
ra_input_pattern: γ_{F}(π_{P}(R)) where F contains aggregate calls with FILTER conditions
ra_output_pattern: γ_{F'}(π_{P'}(R)) where F' has no filters and P' includes CASE expressions derived from filters
ra_condition: Aggregate operator is directly above a Project operator, and at least one AggregateCall has a filter
correctness_conditions: Aggregate functions must ignore NULL values for the CASE transformation to be semantically equivalent to FILTER
Filter condition columns must be available in the input to the Project operator
For DISTINCT aggregates, only COUNT is transformed to ensure reversibility with AggregateCaseToFilterRule
Aggregates with multiple arguments (excluding COUNT) are not transformed to maintain simplicity and correctness
performance_impact: Beneficial for execution engines lacking native FILTER support or optimizing CASE-based aggregation better. May introduce slight overhead due to an additional Project layer and CASE evaluation if native FILTER execution paths are highly optimized.
edge_cases: COUNT() with no arguments requires insertion of a dummy literal (0) inside the CASE expression
DISTINCT aggregates other than COUNT are skipped to prevent irreversible transformations
Aggregates with more than one argument (excluding COUNT) are skipped
Rule does not fire if no aggregate calls contain filters
framework_notes: Implemented as a RelOptRule primarily for VolcanoPlanner. Operates during the logical optimization phase. Does not enforce physical traits (distribution/ordering), focusing on logical expression normalization for broader executor compatibility.

summary: Eliminates a Sort operator when the input relation's maximum row count is guaranteed to be small enough that sorting or limiting is unnecessary.
ra_condition: max_rows(R) ≤ (has_order ? 1 : fetch) AND offset = 0
correctness_conditions: Input maximum row count is known and finite
Input maximum row count is less than or equal to the threshold (1 if ordering exists, fetch value if pure limit)
Sort operator does not contain an OFFSET clause
Sort fetch value is not zero
performance_impact: Beneficial by removing sorting overhead (CPU and memory) when data volume is trivial. Potential risk if statistics are inaccurate leading to incorrect plan removal.
edge_cases: Queries with OFFSET clause
Sort with fetch = 0
Input relations with unknown or infinite cardinality statistics
Downstream operators relying on specific ordering that might be lost if input cardinality estimation is wrong
framework_notes: VolcanoPlanner rule extending RelOptRule. Matches Sort class. Operates during logical optimization phase. Removes the Sort node, implicitly satisfying ordering requirements if cardinality is 1.

summary: Transforms a LogicalIntersect operator (distinct) into a combination of Union All, Aggregate, and Filter operations. It supports two variants: one with partial aggregate pushdown to inputs and one without, enabling further optimization via aggregate rules.
ra_condition: Operation is INTERSECT DISTINCT (INTERSECT ALL is not transformed)
correctness_conditions: The INTERSECT operator must not be INTERSECT ALL (intersect.all == false)
Grouping by all input columns preserves row identity and NULL semantics
Count filter ensures the row exists in every input branch
performance_impact: Beneficial when the execution engine lacks efficient native INTERSECT implementations or when aggregate pushdown reduces data volume before Union. May hurt performance if a native hash-based INTERSECT is available and efficient, as the transformation introduces multiple aggregation stages and a Union.
edge_cases: INTERSECT ALL operations are ignored by this rule
Inputs that are already distinct incur redundant aggregation overhead in pushdown variant
Single-input INTERSECT is semantically equivalent to the input but rarely occurs in practice
framework_notes: Implemented as a RelOptRule compatible with VolcanoPlanner and HepPlanner. Matches LogicalIntersect. Operates in the logical optimization phase before physical trait enforcement. The pushdown variant creates opportunities for AggregateMergeRule and ReduceDecomposableAggregateFunctionsRule.

summary: JoinProjectTransposeRule transforms a Join operator with Project operators on its inputs into a structure where the Join operates directly on the underlying relations, with a combined Project on top. This eliminates intermediate projection layers and allows the join to work with base columns.
ra_condition: Projects do not contain window functions (OVER clause); for outer joins, projections must be null-preserving (strong) on null-generating sides
correctness_conditions: Projection expressions must not contain window/analytic functions (OVER clause)
For outer joins, projections on null-generating sides must be strong (cannot filter out nulls semantically)
Join condition must be preservable after projection merging
Column references in projections must be correctly adjusted after join restructure
performance_impact: Beneficial when it reduces intermediate result sizes by pushing column selection closer to data sources, enables better join column pruning, and allows subsequent optimization rules to work on simpler structures. May hurt performance if the combined projection becomes complex or if the original projects provided beneficial buffering for expensive computations.
edge_cases: Outer joins with null-sensitive projection expressions
Projections containing window functions (OVER clause) are skipped
Correlated subqueries in projection expressions
Joins where one side has no project (asymmetric patterns)
Full outer joins requiring careful null handling on both sides
framework_notes: This is a VolcanoPlanner rule (extends RelOptRule) that operates during logical optimization phase. It uses the Volcano iterator-based matching system via onMatch(). The rule respects the trait system through join.copy() preserving trait sets. It works with RelBuilder for constructing new plan nodes and RexProgram for merging projection expressions. The includeOuter config flag controls whether outer joins are handled.

summary: This rule flattens nested set operations (Union, Intersect, Minus) into a single operator with multiple inputs, reducing plan depth and operator count.
ra_input_pattern: (R op S) op T or R op (S op T)
ra_condition: op ∈ {∪, ∩} OR (op = − AND nested on left), AND (¬top.all ∨ bottom.all)
correctness_conditions: Inputs must be union-compatible (same number of columns and compatible types)
For Union and Intersect, associativity allows merging any nested child
For Minus, only the left input can be merged due to non-associativity on the right
If the parent operator is ALL, the child operator must also be ALL; if parent is DISTINCT, child can be ANY
performance_impact: Generally beneficial by reducing tree depth and number of physical operators. Simplifies the plan for downstream rules like predicate pushdown. Minimal risk of performance degradation.
edge_cases: Minus operations cannot merge the right-hand nested set operation
Cannot merge if parent is ALL and child is DISTINCT
Single-input set operations (though rare) do not trigger merging
framework_notes: Implemented as a RelOptRule in Calcite's Volcano framework. Operates on Logical plan nodes. Uses RelBuilder to reconstruct the flattened operator while preserving traits. Typically runs during logical optimization phase before physical planning.

summary: This rule rewrites an aggregate operation over a star table scan to utilize a pre-computed materialized aggregate table (lattice tile) if one exists that covers the requested grouping and measures, potentially adding a roll-up aggregate or projection to match the original query semantics.
ra_condition: ∃ R_mat ∈ Lattice where R_mat.groupSet ⊇ G and R_mat.measures cover M
correctness_conditions: Materializations must be enabled in CalciteConnectionConfig
The planner must have a registered RelOptLattice for the star table
Aggregation functions must support roll-up (e.g., DISTINCT aggregates cannot be rolled up)
The materialized table's schema must be mappable to the original aggregate's output schema
The rule is disabled during recursive materialization population to prevent loops
performance_impact: Highly beneficial when the materialized table is significantly smaller than the base fact table, reducing I/O and aggregation CPU cost. Potentially neutral or negative if the materialized view access cost is high or if the selectivity of the remaining filter/aggregation is low compared to the base scan.
edge_cases: Queries using DISTINCT aggregates often cannot use rolled-up materializations
Queries requesting groupings not covered by any lattice tile will not match
Recursive materialization creation processes disable this rule to avoid infinite loops
Complex measure mappings may require additional projection layers adding overhead
framework_notes: Compatible with VolcanoPlanner for cost-based optimization; functions as a logical equivalence rule. It relies on the presence of a RelOptLattice in the planner context. It preserves input traits (distribution, collation) when copying the aggregate node. It replaces the base TableScan with a materialized TableScan, allowing the cost model to evaluate the smaller data source.

summary: This rule eliminates explicit Sort operators when the required ordering is already guaranteed by the input relation's collation traits, provided the Sort does not handle LIMIT or OFFSET logic.
ra_condition: offset = \emptyset \land fetch = \emptyset \land \text{collation trait is active}
correctness_conditions: The planner must track RelCollationTraitDef as an active trait
The Sort operator must not contain OFFSET or FETCH (LIMIT) clauses
The input relation must be capable of satisfying the required collation trait
performance_impact: Highly beneficial when data is already sorted (e.g., via index scan), eliminating expensive physical sort operations. No negative impact as it relies on planner's ability to satisfy traits.
edge_cases: Queries with LIMIT/OFFSET clauses cannot have the Sort removed as the Sort node enforces row limiting
Scenarios where no input path can satisfy the collation trait may prevent rule application
framework_notes: VolcanoPlanner rule relying on the Trait system. It converts the input to a RelTraitSet including the required RelCollation, effectively pushing the sorting requirement down as a physical property rather than an operator.

summary: Inserts cast relations (projections) above input nodes to ensure their row types match the expected input types of a consumer relational operator.
ra_condition: ∃i such that RowType(Rᵢ) ≠ ExpectedInputType(Φ, i)
correctness_conditions: Input column data types must be castable to expected types according to SQL semantics
Nullability constraints must be compatible between input and expected types
Column names must match expected names if coerceNames configuration is true
performance_impact: Adds minimal CPU overhead for type casting operations; prevents runtime type mismatch errors; may slightly increase plan depth but ensures logical correctness before physical planning.
edge_cases: Inputs already matching expected types (rule becomes a no-op)
Incompatible types that cannot be implicitly or explicitly cast
Cases where column name coercion is disabled but names differ
framework_notes: Operates on Convention.NONE (logical plan); compatible with both VolcanoPlanner and HepPlanner; executes during logical optimization phase prior to physical trait enforcement; preserves input trait sets.

summary: This rule merges two consecutive Sort operators into a single Sort operator. It specifically optimizes scenarios where the parent Sort acts as a pure limit (no re-ordering) and both Sorts define a literal fetch limit, calculating the minimum of the two limits to push the restriction closer to the data source.
ra_condition: Top Sort is pure limit (no collation change), both fetches are literals, no offsets present
correctness_conditions: The top Sort must not change the ordering (pure limit)
Both fetch values must be non-null literals
Neither Sort should have an offset defined
The bottom Sort's collation is preserved in the output
performance_impact: Beneficial by reducing operator count and allowing the execution engine to stop scanning/sorting earlier based on the smaller limit value. Negligible negative impact.
edge_cases: Queries using dynamic parameters for LIMIT instead of literals
Queries involving OFFSET clauses
Cases where the top Sort re-orders data (different collation)
One or both Sort nodes lack a fetch limit
framework_notes: Used in VolcanoPlanner during logical optimization (RBO). It matches LogicalSort nodes. It preserves the Collation physical trait from the bottom Sort, ensuring compatibility with downstream operators requiring sorted input.

summary: This rule collapses a Union operator whose inputs are all Values operators into a single Values operator containing the aggregated tuples.
ra_condition: All inputs V_i are Values operators with compatible row types
correctness_conditions: Every input to the Union must be a Values operator (or wrapper)
Row types of all inputs must be structurally equivalent to the Union's row type
Duplicate tuples must be removed if the Union operates in distinct mode (all=false)
performance_impact: Generally positive by reducing plan complexity and execution overhead; potential negative impact if the resulting Values list becomes excessively large for memory
edge_cases: Union inputs include non-constant relations (e.g., TableScan)
Input row types differ in field count or data types
Distinct Union requires efficient deduplication of literal tuples
framework_notes: VolcanoPlanner rule applied during logical optimization; selects ImmutableSet builder for distinct unions to handle deduplication automatically

summary: This rule transforms a LogicalTableScan into the relational expression defined by the underlying table, primarily enabling view expansion. It replaces the scan node with the result of the table's toRel() method, allowing the optimizer to see inside view definitions for further optimization.
ra_condition: T is a view defined by expression E_{view}
correctness_conditions: The view definition E_{view} must be semantically equivalent to the table reference T
Column names and types in E_{view} must match T
No circular view dependencies exist in the schema
performance_impact: Beneficial for enabling predicate pushdown and join reordering across view boundaries; may increase plan complexity and optimization time for large view definitions.
edge_cases: Base tables without view definitions (may just convert convention)
Materialized views requiring specific rewrite rules
Recursive views which are generally not supported
framework_notes: Belongs to VolcanoPlanner; executes during early logical optimization phase; interacts with trait system by potentially changing the node's Convention based on the expanded relational tree.

summary: Transforms INTERSECT operations (without ALL) into equivalent EXISTS subquery chains, using the first input as base and filtering with correlated EXISTS conditions for each subsequent input.
ra_condition: INTERSECT without ALL modifier, all inputs have compatible schemas
correctness_conditions: INTERSECT must not use ALL modifier (intersect.all = false)
All input relations must have identical column count and compatible types
Correlation IDs must be unique and properly scoped per subquery
NULL semantics must be preserved using IS NOT DISTINCT FROM comparison
Final DISTINCT operation ensures duplicate elimination matches INTERSECT semantics
performance_impact: Beneficial when EXISTS subqueries can leverage indexes on correlation columns or when query optimizer can unnest correlations efficiently; may hurt performance if INTERSECT has specialized hash-based implementations or when multiple EXISTS checks create correlated nested loop patterns that cannot be decorrelated
edge_cases: INTERSECT ALL operations are not transformed (rule returns early)
Single input INTERSECT is degenerate but handled
Inputs with NULL values require IS NOT DISTINCT FROM semantics for correct matching
Very large number of inputs creates deeply nested EXISTS conditions increasing plan complexity
Type casting required when input field types differ slightly from intersect row type
framework_notes: VolcanoPlanner rule extending RelOptRule; logical transformation phase (RBO); uses RelBuilder for plan construction; creates CorrelationId per subquery; matches LogicalIntersect operand; interacts with trait system through RelNode stripping and type preservation

summary: This rule pushes filter conditions down through a Window operator if the conditions reference only the window's partition keys, splitting the filter into one below the window to reduce input cardinality and one above for remaining conditions.
ra_condition: cols(p_push) ⊆ keys(g) AND window has exactly one group
correctness_conditions: Pushed predicates must reference only columns defined in the Window partition keys
The Window operator must contain exactly one grouping set
Filtering on partition keys before windowing preserves window semantics for remaining rows
performance_impact: Beneficial when predicates on partition keys are selective, reducing the volume of data processed by the expensive Window operator; negligible overhead otherwise.
edge_cases: Window operators with multiple grouping sets (not supported by this rule)
Filter conditions referencing non-partition columns (remain above window)
Cases where pushed conditions are empty (transformation skipped)
framework_notes: VolcanoPlanner rule extending RelOptRule; uses RelBuilder for plan construction; splits predicates based on partition key dependency; ensures single window group constraint.

summary: This rule uses the Dphyp (Dynamic Programming Hybrid) algorithm to find an optimal join ordering for a HyperGraph representation of multiple joins, transforming the join tree to a more efficient execution plan while maintaining semantic equivalence.
ra_output_pattern: Rₚ₁ ⋈ Rₚ₂ ⋈ ... ⋈ Rₚₙ (reordered join tree where p is permutation)
ra_condition: Joins must be semantically reorderable; INNER JOINs fully reorderable, OUTER JOINs have ordering constraints
correctness_conditions: Join semantics must be preserved (INNER JOINs are commutative and associative)
Outer join ordering constraints must be respected (preserving null-producing behavior)
Predicate conditions must remain valid after reordering
The HyperGraph must accurately represent join relationships and constraints
performance_impact: Significant performance improvement for multi-table joins by finding optimal join order; however, the Dphyp enumeration itself has exponential complexity O(2^n) limited by bloat parameter (default 127 for n=7 tables)
edge_cases: Queries with many OUTER JOINs have limited reordering freedom
Very large number of tables (>7 with default bloat) may not be fully optimized
Queries with join predicates that reference all tables equally may have fewer optimization opportunities
Cross joins (cartesian products) may explode search space
framework_notes: VolcanoPlanner rule (uses RelOptRuleCall/transformTo pattern); operates during logical optimization phase; matches HyperGraph operand which represents join relationships; bloat parameter limits DP table size to control optimization time

summary: Transforms a LEFT_MARK join followed by Filter and Project into SEMI or ANTI join based on how the filter condition references the marker column, eliminating the need for explicit marker column handling
ra_input_pattern: π_attrs(σ_(p ∧ m)(R ⟕_q S)) where m is marker column
ra_output_pattern: π_attrs(σ_p(R ⋉_q S)) for SEMI or π_attrs(σ_p(R ▷_q S)) for ANTI
ra_condition: Project excludes marker, Filter references marker as simple predicate (m or ¬m), join condition non-strong for ANTI
correctness_conditions: Project columns must not include the marker column (markIndex)
Filter condition must reference the marker column
For SEMI: marker condition must be direct reference (truthy check)
For ANTI: marker condition must be NOT(marker) with non-strong join condition
Join condition must not propagate NULLs for ANTI transformation (isJoinConditionNotStrong)
Filter conditions on marker must be conjunctive (no disjunctions)
performance_impact: SEMI/ANTI joins are significantly more efficient than LEFT_MARK joins as they enable early termination during join evaluation, avoid materializing marker column, and reduce memory footprint. Particularly beneficial for EXISTS/NOT EXISTS subquery patterns and IN/NOT IN predicates.
edge_cases: Project references marker column (transformation blocked)
Filter does not reference marker column (transformation blocked)
Complex marker conditions (disjunctions, comparisons other than truthy/NOT)
Strong join conditions with NOT(marker) pattern (NULL propagation risk)
Multiple conflicting marker conditions (both SEMI and ANTI patterns)
Marker column used in other expressions beyond simple reference
framework_notes: Volcano-style rule using RelOptRuleCall pattern matching. Operates on logical relational algebra before physical operator selection. Part of Calcite's VolcanoPlanner logical optimization phase. Uses RelBuilder for plan reconstruction. Rule matching enforced through Config.operandSupplier chain (Project→Filter→Join with LEFT_MARK type).

summary: Converts a LogicalProject operator into a LogicalCalc operator by encapsulating the projection logic within a RexProgram. This transformation normalizes the plan, enabling subsequent optimizations like merging filters and projects into a single calculation node.
ra_condition: expressions contain no subqueries and no match-recognize logic
correctness_conditions: The RexProgram must correctly evaluate all projection expressions
Input row type must match the RexProgram's expected input schema
No correlated subqueries exist within the projection expressions
No MATCH_RECOGNIZE specific logic is present in the expressions
performance_impact: Beneficial for enabling operator fusion (e.g., combining Filter and Project into one Calc), reducing iteration overhead over rows. Prepares the plan for efficient code generation. Neutral if no subsequent fusion rules apply.
edge_cases: Projections containing correlated subqueries (excluded by rule predicate)
Projections involving MATCH_RECOGNIZE logic (excluded by rule predicate)
Identity projections where overhead of Calc might outweigh benefits without fusion
framework_notes: VolcanoPlanner rule (RelOptRule). Operates on Logical nodes (Convention.NONE). Does not enforce physical traits. Typically applied during logical optimization phase to prepare for physical planning.

summary: This rule reorders inner joins by pushing one child of a parent join through a child join, transforming the join tree topology (e.g., from (A⋈B)⋈C to (A⋈C)⋈B) to explore different join orders for cost optimization.
ra_condition: Both joins are INNER JOINs; join conditions can be split based on column references from the swapped relation
correctness_conditions: Both joins must be INNER JOIN type to preserve null semantics
Join conditions must be decomposable into conjuncts referencing specific input relations
Output schema must be preserved via permutation mapping (Project added if column order changes)
Predicate logic must remain equivalent after splitting and recombining conditions
performance_impact: Beneficial for enabling cost-based optimizer to find better join orders (e.g. joining selective tables early); may increase planning time due to search space expansion and adds a Project operator that requires subsequent pruning
edge_cases: Outer joins (LEFT, RIGHT, FULL) are excluded by rule check
Join conditions that cannot be split (e.g., complex UDFs referencing all tables)
Cases where no predicates can be pushed down (rule aborts if nonIntersecting is empty)
Semi-joins or Anti-joins (handled differently or excluded)
framework_notes: Used in VolcanoPlanner for cost-based optimization; interacts with trait sets via RelNode.copy; generates a Project to maintain schema compatibility which may be optimized away by ProjectMergeRule later

summary: This rule transforms an Aggregate-Project pattern where the Project contains constant literals by extracting those constants into a separate VALUES relation and joining it with the original input, making constants explicit for downstream optimization.
ra_condition: At least one expression in Project E is a RexLiteral (constant) that is referenced by Aggregate group keys
correctness_conditions: The VALUES table contains exactly one row with the constant values
The join is INNER JOIN which preserves cardinality with a single-row table
Project expressions are correctly remapped to reference the joined constant columns
Aggregate group set and aggregate calls remain semantically equivalent
Field offsets are correctly calculated after the join operation
performance_impact: Beneficial when it enables downstream optimizations like constant folding, predicate pushdown, or partition pruning. May add minor overhead if the join is not optimized away by subsequent rules, but typically the single-row VALUES join is eliminated during physical planning.
edge_cases: Queries with no constant expressions in the Project will not match
Constants not referenced by aggregate group keys may not trigger the rule
May not help if downstream optimizers cannot exploit the explicit constant representation
Complex expressions mixing constants and columns are not extracted, only pure RexLiteral nodes
framework_notes: VolcanoPlanner rule using RelBuilder pattern. Operates on logical plan before physical optimization. Does not directly interact with trait system as it preserves logical equivalence. Rule matches Aggregate with Project as direct input using operand supplier configuration.

summary: Transforms a left-associative join tree (A ⋈ B) ⋈ C into a right-associative tree A ⋈ (B ⋈ C) to enable alternative join orderings during optimization.
ra_condition: Both joins are INNER JOINs; relations A and C share the same convention; join conditions are conjunctive and splittable based on column references
correctness_conditions: Both top and bottom joins must be INNER JOINs
Relation A and Relation C must have matching conventions (traits)
Top join must not contain system fields
Join conditions must be redistributable based on column references without changing semantics
Resulting join conditions must not be always TRUE if allowAlwaysTrueCondition is disabled
performance_impact: Expands the optimization search space for join ordering. Beneficial when the cardinality of B ⋈ C is lower than A ⋈ B, reducing intermediate result size. May increase planning time due to additional plan alternatives generated.
edge_cases: Outer joins (LEFT, RIGHT, FULL) are not supported and will block the rule
Joins with system fields are excluded
Mismatched conventions between input relations prevent transformation
If resulting join conditions become always TRUE and allowAlwaysTrueCondition is false, rule aborts
framework_notes: Belongs to VolcanoPlanner. Operates during logical optimization phase to expand join order search space. Interacts with trait system by verifying convention compatibility between outer relations (A and C) before transformation.

summary: This rule pushes an Aggregate operator down through a Union All operator. It transforms a query that aggregates the result of a union into a query that aggregates each union input individually and then aggregates the combined results, enabling parallel aggregation.
ra_condition: Union is UNION ALL, Aggregates are decomposable (SUM, COUNT, MIN, MAX), no DISTINCT aggregates
correctness_conditions: The Union operator must be UNION ALL (duplicates preserved)
Aggregate functions must be decomposable (e.g., SUM, COUNT, MIN, MAX)
Aggregate calls cannot use DISTINCT
Grouping columns must not be unique keys in all union inputs to prevent infinite optimization loops
Nullability of aggregate results must be adjusted if input column nullability differs from union output
performance_impact: Beneficial for parallelizing aggregation across union inputs and reducing data volume before the final aggregation step. May hurt performance if inputs are small or if grouping columns are already unique in inputs, adding unnecessary operator overhead.
edge_cases: UNION DISTINCT (semantics change for SUM/COUNT)
COUNT(DISTINCT col) or other DISTINCT aggregates
Unsupported aggregate functions (not in SUPPORTED_AGGREGATES)
Grouping columns form a unique key in all union inputs
framework_notes: Belongs to VolcanoPlanner as a logical transformation rule (RelOptRule). It matches an Aggregate relational expression on top of a Union relational expression. It operates on logical plan structure without explicitly enforcing physical traits, relying on the builder to handle type mapping and field indexing.

summary: Merges a Filter operator into a downstream Calc operator by combining their RexPrograms, effectively pushing the filter predicate into the Calc's internal logic to reduce plan depth and operator count.
ra_condition: Calc does not contain window functions (ω)
correctness_conditions: The Calc operator must not contain windowed aggregates (containsOver() returns false)
The Filter condition must not contain subqueries (enforced by operand predicate in Config)
The merged RexProgram must maintain semantic equivalence of expression evaluation order
performance_impact: Beneficial by reducing operator count and enabling expression simplification within a single node; avoids overhead of separate filter evaluation. No significant negative impact unless it prevents other rules from firing (rare).
edge_cases: Calc operators containing window functions (OVER clauses)
Filters containing subqueries (excluded by rule configuration)
Scenarios where separate Filter node is required for specific trait propagation
framework_notes: Executes in VolcanoPlanner during logical optimization. Preserves trait set of the Calc operator. Prevents merging if window functions exist to avoid changing evaluation order relative to window computation.
